name: Vyasa (Yoga commentator)
dates_notes: The author of the Yoga-Sūtra-Bhāṣya is conventionally named Vyāsa but is almost certainly distinct from the legendary Veda-Vyāsa; Maas (2013, 'A Concise Historiography of Classical Yoga Philosophy') argues for treating sūtras and bhāṣya together as a single composite work — Pātañjala-Yoga-Śāstra — with the bhāṣya as Patañjali's auto-commentary or contemporaneous redaction. Larson follows this. The historical Vyāsa-as-distinct-author hypothesis is the older view (Garbe; Wezler) and remains alive. Dates given here cover both readings.
core_thesis: The *Yoga-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* — the *Pātañjala-Yoga-Śāstra*'s only intra-traditional commentary — holds Sāṃkhya metaphysics within the Yoga frame and discharges what the *sūtras* leave compressed. The metaphysics is Sāṃkhya: twenty-five *tattvas*, *puruṣa*-*prakṛti* dualism, the three *guṇas* as constitutive of *prakṛti* (*Yoga-Bhāṣya* on Yoga-Sūtra 2.18–19), *satkārya-vāda* on causation (*Yoga-Bhāṣya* on YS 4.12). The Yoga additions are doctrinally consequential. *Citta-vṛtti-nirodha* — the cessation of mental modifications — is unfolded as a graded analysis of the five *vṛttis* (*Yoga-Bhāṣya* on YS 1.5–11), the four *samādhis* of the first *pāda* (on YS 1.17, 1.42–46), and the eight *aṅgas* of *kriyā-yoga* in the second (on YS 2.29 ff.). *Īśvara* is admitted as a distinct *puruṣa-viśeṣa* untouched by *kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya* (*Yoga-Bhāṣya* on YS 1.24): not a creator, but a perfect contemplative *puruṣa* whose *praṇidhāna* is one efficient route to *samādhi* (on YS 1.23 and 2.45). Two technical contributions are systemically important. First, the analysis of *kaivalya* in the fourth *pāda* as the *guṇa*'s reabsorption into their source (*Yoga-Bhāṣya* on YS 4.32–34): liberation is the *puruṣa*'s isolation from *prakṛti* once the *guṇas* have completed their work for that *puruṣa*. Second, the *bhāṣya*'s detailed phenomenology of *samādhi*, *dhyāna*, and *dhāraṇā* (*Yoga-Bhāṣya* on YS 3.1–4 and 3.9–11), which becomes the standard description of yogic praxis in subsequent Indian thought. The *bhāṣya* is the base text on which Vāchaspati Miśra (*Tattva-Vaiśāradī*) and Vijñānabhikṣu (*Yoga-Vārttika*) build the standard pre-modern reading of Patañjali.

engaged_works.title: Yoga-Sutra-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Dating depends on the composite-text question; the conventional fifth-century date stands either way.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa; modern scholarship (Maas 2013) increasingly treats the sūtras-with-bhāṣya as a single Pātañjala-Yoga-Śāstra. Either way the bhāṣya is the only intra-traditional commentary and is the base text for all later Yoga scholasticism.

ascription_evidence.description: Author-identity debate: Maas (2013) defends single-authorship; Wezler and Bronkhorst defend distinct-bhāṣyakāra Vyāsa.

engaged_works.summary: Prose commentary on Patañjali's *Yoga-Sūtra* across all four *pādas* — *samādhi*, *sādhana*, *vibhūti*, *kaivalya*. The *bhāṣya* installs the Sāṃkhya twenty-five-*tattva* ontology as the metaphysical frame within which Yoga praxis is intelligible. The first *pāda* receives the most exegetically dense treatment: *yogaḥ citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ* (on YS 1.2) opens out into a typology of the five *vṛttis* (on YS 1.5–11), an analysis of *abhyāsa* and *vairāgya* (on YS 1.12–16), and the doctrine of *samprajñāta* and *asamprajñāta-samādhi* (on YS 1.17–18). The second *pāda* unfolds *kriyā-yoga* (on YS 2.1) and the eight *aṅgas* — *yama* (YS 2.30), *niyama* (YS 2.32), *āsana* (YS 2.46), *prāṇāyāma* (YS 2.49), *pratyāhāra* (YS 2.54), with *dhāraṇā*, *dhyāna*, *samādhi* deferred to the third *pāda*. The third *pāda* on *vibhūtis* treats the *saṃyama*-generated powers (on YS 3.16–48), with the warning at YS 3.37 that they obstruct *samādhi*. The fourth *pāda* on *kaivalya* gives the cleanest statement of the Yoga *mokṣa*-doctrine: liberation as the *puruṣa*'s isolation from *prakṛti* once the *guṇas* have been reabsorbed (on YS 4.32–34). Vāchaspati Miśra's *Tattva-Vaiśāradī* and Vijñānabhikṣu's *Yoga-Vārttika* are the two principal pre-modern sub-commentaries.
